I watched the whole series. It’s definitely a PR-driven project, designed to highlight Victoria’s image as a serious entrepreneur and reinforce the credibility behind her brand. Not that she really needed it, but after some financial turbulence, keeping the conversation positive around her business — even through well-crafted documentaries like this — surely helps.

Of course, there’s also a clear intention to show a strong and supportive family image, which makes her more relatable. But what truly deserves praise is Victoria’s genuine belief in her work. She clearly has talent, she’s resilient, and she keeps moving forward despite the challenges that come with the fashion industry — which, as she rightly points out in the docuseries, is a completely different world from entertainment, with greater responsibilities and financial risks.

One last note: I really appreciated the respect she showed towards the Spice Girls, especially the fact that the final song playing through the end credits is from their first album. It feels like a beautiful acknowledgment that her current success still stems from that incredible starting point.

I’m so glad that Victoria had the opportunity to share her experience about carving out her own name in the fashion industry before someone else tries to "steal" her story. With Meghan Markle’s recent appearance at Paris Fashion Week, it’s becoming clear that she’s attempting to enter the fashion world herself, seemingly following in Victoria’s footsteps with her own line. Meghan’s previous ventures — from her lifestyle and food projects to her Netflix show — haven’t made the impact she hoped for. The difference lies in authenticity: Victoria exudes humility, warmth, and genuine passion, whereas Meghan often comes across as overly scripted, calculated, and performative.

It’s also worth noting that Meghan appears to be emulating Victoria’s signature style lately — from her reserved facial expressions on the red carpet to her tailored blazers, slacks, and trademark hand-in-pocket pose. These are unmistakably Victoria’s trademarks, not Meghan’s. She may attempt to borrow Victoria’s aesthetic in an effort to reinvent herself, but imitation rarely captures the essence of the original.

Victoria and David Beckham embody grace and sophistication. They embraced the California lifestyle — complete with chicken coops and beekeeping — long before Meghan and Harry arrived on the scene. Their natural connection to both the Royal Family and Hollywood circles gives them a standing that Meghan will likely never attain. And let’s not forget: Victoria was a global icon well before meeting David. Meghan, before Harry? Not quite.
